fast.summary: This tutorial paper surveys few-shot learning methods for graph-structured data, organizing approaches into problem taxonomies (data scarcity types) and technique taxonomies (meta-learning, pre-LLM pre-training, and LLM-era pre-training). It provides a comparative analysis of state-of-the-art methods and outlines future research directions for learning on graphs with minimal labeled data.
fast.key_idea: By combining meta-learning and LLM-empowered pre-training strategies, models can generalize effectively on graphs even when labeled training examples are scarce.
fast.why_it_matters: In real-world applications like recommendation systems and social networks, obtaining large labeled datasets is expensive; few-shot graph learning enables practitioners to build high-quality models with minimal annotation effort.
possible_use_cases: Cold-start recommendation: predicting preferences for new users or items with no interaction history
Drug discovery: predicting properties of novel molecular graphs with few known examples
Fraud detection: identifying rare fraud patterns in transaction graphs with limited labeled fraud cases

deep.method_breakdown: This is a tutorial/survey paper that organizes few-shot learning on graphs into two taxonomies: a problem taxonomy covering data scarcity types (node classification, link prediction, graph classification under low-label regimes) and a technique taxonomy covering meta-learning (MAML, prototypical networks adapted to graphs) and pre-training approaches (GNN pre-training before LLMs, and LLM-empowered pre-training leveraging language model knowledge). The survey traces the evolution from task-specific meta-learning methods that learn to learn across episodes, to generalist pre-trained graph models that transfer structural and semantic knowledge to new tasks with minimal labeled examples.
deep.key_innovation: The primary contribution is a unified dual-taxonomy framework that bridges three historically separate paradigms — graph meta-learning, pre-LLM graph pre-training (e.g., GraphCL, GPT-GNN), and LLM-empowered approaches (e.g., using LLMs as feature encoders or graph reasoners) — under a single few-shot learning lens. Prior surveys treated these as disjoint areas; this work identifies shared principles and contrasts their assumptions about what constitutes transferable knowledge (task-agnostic structure vs. semantic node/edge features).
deep.technical_insight: The most critical design insight is the distinction between structural few-shot transfer (meta-learning on subgraph episodes, exploiting local graph topology) and semantic few-shot transfer (LLM-based pre-training that encodes textual node attributes). Graph meta-learning excels when topology is the discriminative signal but fails on heterophilic or text-rich graphs, whereas LLM-empowered pre-training handles semantic richness but can ignore relational structure — the open challenge is combining both without catastrophic interference.
deep.limitations: As a survey/tutorial, it inherits the limitations of the field it covers: (1) most benchmarks are homophilic citation or molecular graphs, so findings may not generalize to heterophilic or dynamic graphs; (2) LLM-empowered methods are computationally expensive and depend on the quality of textual node attributes, which many real-world graphs lack; (3) the few-shot evaluation protocol varies widely across papers (K-shot N-way vs. percentage-labeled), making cross-paper comparison unreliable; (4) the survey may not fully resolve how to combine structural and semantic inductive biases in a principled way.
interview_questions: What is the fundamental difference between meta-learning-based and pre-training-based approaches to few-shot graph learning, and when would you prefer one over the other?
How does a prototypical network for graphs work, and what graph-specific challenges arise compared to image-based few-shot learning?
What role do LLMs play in LLM-empowered graph pre-training, and what are the risks of relying on LLMs for graph tasks?
Why is the few-shot evaluation protocol (e.g., N-way K-shot) potentially misleading for graph tasks, and how would you design a more robust benchmark?
How would you handle a cold-start node classification problem on a graph with no textual attributes and only 2 labeled nodes per class?
interview_answers: Meta-learning (e.g., MAML, ProtoNet on graphs) learns an episodic training objective that simulates few-shot tasks, making it fast at test-time adaptation but requiring many meta-training tasks from the same distribution. Pre-training (e.g., GraphCL, LLM-based) learns transferable representations from large unlabeled corpora, performing better when the target domain has rich unlabeled data but fewer related labeled tasks. Prefer meta-learning when labeled tasks are plentiful but per-task labels are scarce; prefer pre-training when large unlabeled graphs exist but meta-tasks cannot be constructed.

A prototypical network for graphs computes a class prototype as the mean embedding of K support nodes (encoded by a GNN), then classifies query nodes by nearest-prototype distance in embedding space. Graph-specific challenges include: (1) message passing means support and query node embeddings are not independent — neighbor leakage can inflate performance; (2) graph topology must be included in the episode but subgraph sampling strategy dramatically affects results; (3) heterophily means GNN embeddings can mix class signals, making prototypes less separable.

LLMs serve as semantic encoders for node textual features (e.g., paper abstracts, product descriptions), providing rich zero-shot or few-shot priors that pure GNNs lack. They can also act as graph reasoners via prompting (e.g., describing neighborhood structure in text). Risks include: hallucination of graph structure properties, high inference cost making transductive use impractical, over-reliance on text quality so graphs without meaningful textual attributes get poor embeddings, and potential distribution shift between LLM pretraining corpus and domain-specific graph data.

Standard N-way K-shot evaluation samples random subgraphs, ignoring that graph nodes are not i.i.d. — nearby nodes share neighbors, inflating accuracy via structural leakage. A more robust benchmark would: (1) enforce strict inductive splits where test nodes have no edges to training nodes during support-set encoding; (2) test across multiple graph domains to measure true transfer; (3) vary graph density and homophily to expose method sensitivity; (4) report variance across many random seeds since small K makes results highly variable.

With no textual attributes and only 2 labeled nodes per class, I would use a graph meta-learning approach: (1) pre-train a GNN on auxiliary graph tasks (e.g., link prediction, graph-level self-supervised objectives like GraphCL contrastive learning) to learn transferable structural embeddings; (2) at inference time, construct a prototypical network where class prototypes are the GNN embeddings of the 2 labeled nodes; (3) classify new nodes by embedding similarity; (4) optionally augment support set via neighborhood aggregation to create soft pseudo-labeled nodes from high-confidence 1-hop neighbors.
